instructions: Speak like a friendly, confident cybersecurity expert explaining to a colleague. Clear and measured pace, natural emphasis on key terms, calm and authoritative — not rushed, not robotic.
intro: AWS Organizations lets you manage many accounts as one, and Service Control Policies — SCPs — are the guardrails that set the maximum permissions any account can use. Let's walk through the org structure and how to write SCPs that hold the line.

bullets: The management account sits at the root; member accounts are grouped into Organizational Units, or OUs.
OUs let you apply policy to whole groups of accounts — Prod, Dev, Sandbox, Security.
Keep the management account nearly empty; run workloads in member accounts only.
sections.narration: Start with the shape of the organization. At the top is the management account, sitting at the root. Below it, member accounts are grouped into organizational units, or OUs — think Prod, Dev, Sandbox, and Security. OUs are what let you apply policy to a whole group of accounts at once. One rule to remember: keep the management account almost empty and run your real workloads in member accounts, because it's a powerful blast radius you don't want exposed.

sections.cardTitle: SCPs set the MAXIMUM, not the grant
bullets: An SCP defines the ceiling of available permissions — it never grants access by itself.
Effective permissions = the intersection of the SCP and the account's IAM policies.
SCPs do not apply to the management account, and never to the service-linked roles AWS manages.
sections.narration: Here's the single most important idea: a service control policy is a guardrail, not a grant. It sets the maximum permissions an account can ever use, but it never gives access on its own — you still need IAM policies inside the account. The effective permissions are the intersection of the two: something is allowed only if both the SCP and IAM allow it. And note two exceptions — SCPs don't restrict the management account, and they don't touch the service-linked roles AWS manages.

sections.kicker: Two ways to draw the line
bullets: Deny-list (most common): start from FullAWSAccess and add explicit Deny statements to carve out the forbidden.
Allow-list: remove FullAWSAccess and explicitly allow only the services you sanction — tighter, but high-maintenance.
An explicit Deny always wins, so deny statements are the strongest control you have.
sections.narration: There are two strategies for writing SCPs. The deny-list approach is the common one: you keep the default FullAWSAccess policy and layer explicit deny statements on top to carve out what's forbidden. The allow-list approach removes FullAWSAccess and explicitly permits only the services you sanction — it's tighter, but much higher maintenance. Whichever you choose, remember that an explicit deny always wins over any allow, which makes deny statements your strongest guardrail.

sections.cardTitle: Common deny SCPs to ship day one
bullets: Restrict regions: deny actions outside your approved regions to shrink the attack surface and meet residency rules.
Block root: deny actions taken by the account root user across member accounts.
Protect logging: deny anyone disabling or deleting CloudTrail, Config, or GuardDuty.
sections.narration: These are the guardrails almost every organization ships on day one. First, restrict regions — deny actions outside the regions you've approved, which shrinks your attack surface and helps with data residency. Second, block the root user — deny actions taken as account root across your member accounts. And third, protect your detective controls — deny anyone from stopping or deleting CloudTrail, Config, or GuardDuty. Lock those down and a compromised account can't quietly turn off the cameras.

drill.cardTitle: What a deny SCP looks like
bullets: Effect 'Deny' on cloudtrail:StopLogging and cloudtrail:DeleteTrail stops anyone from blinding your audit trail.
A Condition like aws:RequestedRegion narrows a region-restriction SCP to exactly the regions you allow.
Test SCPs on a Sandbox OU before the root — a bad deny can lock out everyone, fast.
drill.narration: Here's the actual policy document. This deny statement targets CloudTrail's stop-logging and delete-trail actions, so no one in a member account can blind your audit trail. A region guardrail uses a condition on the requested-region key to allow only the regions you name. One word of caution: always test a new SCP on a sandbox OU before attaching it near the root, because a careless deny can lock everyone out in seconds.

sections.cardTitle: Delegate administration to a member account
bullets: Delegated administration lets a trusted member account manage a service org-wide — without using the management account.
Common for Security: delegate GuardDuty, Security Hub, and IAM Access Analyzer to a dedicated security account.
This keeps day-to-day operations out of the high-privilege management account.
sections.narration: Finally, delegated administration. AWS lets you nominate a trusted member account to manage a service across the whole organization, so your team doesn't have to sign into the management account to do daily work. The classic pattern is a dedicated security account that's the delegated administrator for GuardDuty, Security Hub, and IAM Access Analyzer. This keeps routine operations out of the high-privilege management account, which is exactly where you want them.

card.title: Organizations & SCPs in five rules
lines: 1 · Management account at the root; group accounts into OUs.
2 · SCPs cap the MAXIMUM — they never grant.
3 · Effective = SCP ∩ IAM; explicit Deny always wins.
4 · Ship guardrails: restrict regions, block root, protect logging.
5 · Delegate admin to a security account; keep the management account empty.

recap.narration: In short: put the management account at the root and group your accounts into OUs; remember that SCPs set the maximum and never grant on their own; effective permissions are the intersection of the SCP and IAM, and an explicit deny always wins; ship the core guardrails — restrict regions, block root, and protect your logging services; and delegate administration to a dedicated security account so the management account stays empty. That's Organizations and SCPs, done right.
recap.examTip: Exam tip: SCPs limit the maximum available permissions and never grant access; they don't apply to the management account; and effective permissions are the intersection of SCPs and IAM policies, where an explicit Deny overrides any Allow.

exercises.title: Exercise 1 — Write a region-restriction SCP
lines: GOAL · Prove an SCP caps permissions without granting them
1 · Organizations → create a Sandbox OU and move a test member account in
2 · Policies → create an SCP that denies all actions where aws:RequestedRegion isn't us-east-1
3 · Attach it to the Sandbox OU, then try to launch EC2 in eu-west-1
✓ DONE WHEN · The eu-west-1 action is denied even though IAM allows it
exercises.narration: Time to make this real. In your own lab organization, create a Sandbox OU and move a disposable test account into it. Now write a service control policy that denies every action whose requested region isn't us-east-1, and attach it to the Sandbox OU. Sign into the test account with an admin IAM role and try to launch an EC2 instance in eu-west-1. You're done when that action is denied — even though IAM would have allowed it — which proves the SCP is capping the maximum, not granting anything.

exercises.title: Exercise 2 — Protect your audit trail
lines: GOAL · Make CloudTrail tamper-resistant across the OU
1 · Policies → create an SCP denying cloudtrail:StopLogging and cloudtrail:DeleteTrail
2 · Attach it to the Sandbox OU
3 · In the test account, try to stop or delete a trail as an admin
✓ DONE WHEN · Even an account admin cannot disable CloudTrail
exercises.narration: Second exercise: protect the cameras. Create a service control policy that denies the CloudTrail stop-logging and delete-trail actions, and attach it to your Sandbox OU. Now sign into the test account as an administrator and try to stop or delete a trail. You're done when even a full account admin is blocked — that's the guardrail proving that a compromised account still can't quietly turn off your audit trail.
